title: PopBench: Population-Aware Hierarchical Adaptive Evaluation for LLMs
description: A hierarchical Bayesian IRT framework that models LLMs as samples from a structured population, enabling zero-shot evaluation (predicting capabilities without observing any responses) and cross-model information sharing. Unlike prior adaptive testing methods (ATLAS, M3IRT, Deep-CAT) that treat each model independently, PopBench learns population covariance structure to share information across related models, reducing evaluation cost by 90%+ while providing instant zero-shot rankings.
motivation: Current LLM evaluation treats each model as an independent test-taker, ignoring the strong correlations within model families and the predictable structure of the model population. This leads to redundant evaluation of related models and inefficient cold-start for new models. With thousands of models released annually, we need evaluation that leverages population structure for dramatic efficiency gains.
proposed_approach: 1) Hierarchical Bayesian MIRT: Model individual model capabilities as draws from family-level distributions, which are draws from a global population. Learn covariance matrices capturing relationships across capability dimensions. 2) Metadata-Conditioned Zero-Shot Prediction: Use model metadata (family, size, architecture) to predict initial capabilities via learned scaling relationships—no items required. 3) Population-Aware Adaptive Selection: Select items using hierarchical Expected Information Gain that considers information about both individual and family parameters. 4) Cross-Model Information Sharing: Jointly evaluate related models, sharing family parameters to reduce total items needed.

related_work.paper: ATLAS: Adaptive Testing for LLM Ability Scoring (Li et al., 2025)
related_work.key_difference: ATLAS uses unidimensional IRT with independent models. PopBench uses hierarchical multi-dimensional IRT with learned population structure, enabling zero-shot prediction and cross-model information sharing.

related_work.paper: M3IRT: Multimodal Multidimensional IRT (Uebayashi et al., 2026)
related_work.key_difference: M3IRT decomposes multimodal capabilities but treats models independently. PopBench addresses population structure across models, not modalities, and enables zero-shot evaluation.

related_work.paper: Deep Computerized Adaptive Testing (Li et al., 2025)
related_work.key_difference: Deep-CAT uses deep RL for item selection but evaluates models independently. PopBench uses hierarchical Bayesian modeling to share information across models, enabling population-aware selection.
Classic MAT assumes independent examinees with pre-calibrated items. PopBench introduces hierarchical population priors, online joint estimation, and zero-shot capability prediction from metadata.

Huo et al. share information across pre-specified human study groups for analysis. PopBench learns population structure adaptively, supports real-time adaptive testing, and enables zero-shot prediction for new models.

related_work.paper: PSN-IRT: Neural IRT for LLM Benchmarking (Zhou et al., 2025)
related_work.key_difference: PSN-IRT focuses on analyzing existing benchmarks with neural IRT. PopBench builds on neural IRT for active, adaptive evaluation with population-aware item selection.

hypothesis: Population-aware hierarchical adaptive evaluation can (1) achieve >0.75 Spearman correlation with ground-truth rankings in zero-shot setting (no items observed), (2) reduce evaluation to <10% of items for MAE < 0.05 on models from known families, and (3) achieve 40% item reduction when jointly evaluating related models vs independent evaluation.
success_criteria: Zero-shot Spearman correlation > 0.7 with ground-truth rankings without observing any model responses
Adaptive evaluation achieves MAE < 0.05 using ≤ 10% of benchmark items on held-out models
Joint evaluation of 4+ related models shows >30% total item reduction vs independent evaluation
90% credible intervals contain true performance 85-95% of the time (well-calibrated uncertainty)
Learned scaling relationships achieve R² > 0.9 for predicting model performance from metadata
expected_contribution: First population-aware hierarchical IRT framework for LLM evaluation
Zero-shot capability prediction for new models using learned population structure
Cross-model information sharing for joint evaluation of model families
Population-aware adaptive item selection using hierarchical information gain
Empirical validation of scaling law recovery from learned population parameters
feasibility: Feasible within 8-hour compute budget. Hierarchical model training on historical data takes ~3 hours on A6000. Evaluation uses API calls or local models. No training of LLMs required—only inference and Bayesian posterior computation.
